coredata.dc:title: Carbon-based metal oxide nanocomposites for water treatment by photocatalytic processes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cruz-Quesada, Guillermo
García-Ruíz, Cristian
López-Ramón, María Victoria
Fernández-Poyatos, María del Pilar
Velo-Gala, Inmaculada
coredata.dc:description: The increasing contamination of water by emerging contaminants and the need for more efficient and sustainable treatment methods have prompted the exploration of advanced materials and technologies, with a particular focus on photocatalysis. Carbon-based metal oxide nanocomposites are a promising solution for the treatment of polluted water. This paper aims to review the current state of research on the application of these nanocomposites as photocatalysts for complete water treatment, describing breakthroughs in contaminant removal from 2019 through 2024 and milestones in water disinfection from 2016 through 2024. It includes discussion on the utilization of nanocomposites of Metal Oxides (MOs) with carbon materials to improve photocatalytic efficiency and addresses the advantages and drawbacks of these materials, including electron-hole recombination and agglomeration. The review focuses on the photocatalytic mechanisms of these nanocomposites and highlights the importance of heterostructures formed between metal oxides and carbon materials (e.g., graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots), which enhance light absorption and hydroxyl radical generation, thereby increasing the efficiency of pollutant degradation and water disinfection. The review describes the properties of different MOs (n-type and p-type), exploring synergies between MOs and carbon materials and discussing the benefits and challenges of their application in wastewater treatment and pathogen inactivation. The review ends with a scientometric analysis of research trends in this field.

Composite prepared with the maximum amount of NCQDs achieved 86 % degradation rate of MOr after 275 min of irradiation. Only a loss of 9.0 % in photocatalytic activity was observed after 5 reuse cycles
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/N-TiO2 (30CDS/N-TiO2)
Rhodamine B (RhB) dye

3 g L−1 of catalyst
10 mg L−1 RhB
20 mL reaction
CQDs source: ascorbic acid
Solar light irradiation (λ > 420 nm)

Photoinduced e
– from the CB of N-TiO2 are transferred its VB and then to the CQDs which act as an electron reservoir. Dissolved O2 captures the e
– of CQDs yielding O2
•
− radicals which, together with the h
+ in the VB of N-TiO2, degrade RhB
99.8 % of RhB was removed after 5 min of irradiation. Degradation rate still maintained a value of 94.1 % after 5 reuse cycles
WO3 /NCQDs
Methylene Blue (MB) dye

0.6 g L−1 of catalyst
5 or 50 mg L−1 solution of MB
100 mL of reaction
CQDs source: sugarcane bagasse
80 W visible halogen lamps

Photoinduced e
– from the VB of WO3 are promoted to the CB and then are scavenged by the CQDs and transferred to dissolved O2 generating O2
•
−. Meanwhile the h
+ in the VB of TiO2 react with H2O producing HO• radicals

After 240 min of irradiation, the composite prepared with 2.5 wt% of ethylenediamine removed 96.86 % and 92.93 % of the 5 and 50 mg L−1 MB solutions, respectively. The catalyst was reused in a sequence of 3 cycles of degradation with 86.85 % removal
Carbon dot/TiO2 (CDT) composite. Optimized composite 2CDT was prepared using 2 mL of CDT suspension

100 mL reaction

1000 W Xenon lamp (Simulated Solar light)

– from the HOMO level of the CQDs are promoted to LUMO level and then transferred to the CB of TiO2 where react with dissolved O2 generating O2
•
−, while the h
+ in the VB react with H2O yielding HO• radicals
2 CDT completely degraded PR and AT after 3 h of irradiation and achieved 76 and 86 % mineralization, respectively. 2CDT showed successful degradation up to 3 reuse cycles

N and S co-doped CQDs-modified Bi2O3 binary composite (N,S-CQD- Bi2O3)
RhB, Xylenol Orange (XO), and Sunset Yellow (SY) dyes; 2,4-D and diuron herbicides; and Tetracycline (TC) antibiotic

0.83 g L−1 of catalyst
10 mg L−1 of RhB, or 20 mg L−1 of 2,4-D, diuron or tetracycline
30 mL reaction
CQDs source: methionine and o-phenylenediamine
Natural sunlight

Z-Scheme heterojunction
Photoinduced e
– and h
+ are generated in the CB and VB of Bi2O3, respectively. Photoinduced e
– are then transferred to the CB of N,S-CQD, where they form O2
•
− radicals upon reaction with O2, while the h
+ in the VB produce HO• radicals by reacting with H2O

5 wt% N,S-CQD-Bi2O3 degraded 96 % of RhB, 94 % of XO, and 92 % of SY in 12 min. Additionally, TOC removal of TC was 90 % in 1 h, 2,4-D was 92 % in 3 h, and diuron was 87 % in 2 h. The catalytic activity of the composite remained unchanged after 4 reuse cycles
Fe3O4 @CQDs. Prepared by modifying α-Fe2O3, and used for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation
RhB dye

0.16 g L−1 of catalyst
3 × 10−3 mol L−1 of PMS 20 mg L−1 of RhB
300 mL reaction
CQDs source: glucose
300 W UV Xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm)

Fe2+ activate PMS generating SO4 •– radicals and Fe3+ then, the latter is reduced to Fe2+ by photoelectron interaction under visible light. CQDs capture photoelectrons and accelerate charge transfer. O2
•
−; HO•; 1O2 and h + also contribute to RhB degradation

98 % of RhB was degraded in 35 min (k
app = 0.1253 min−1). The composite was successfully reused in 4 reuse cycles
Cuttlebone powder (CBo) modified with CQDs/nickel oxide (NiO)
Malachite Green (MG) dye

0.3 g L−1 of catalyst
93 mg L−1 of MG pH 6.1 and 36.5 °C
CQDs source: plant, Artemisia Dracunculus
35 W visible halogen lamp

Photoinduced e
– and h
+ are generated in the CB and VB, respectively, of both NiO and CBo. The photoinduced e
– are transferred to the surface of CQDs through Ni–O–C bonds where they react with O2 generating O2
•
− radicals, while the h
+ in the VBs reaction with H2O producing HO• radicals
After 12 min of visible light irradiation, the composite reduced 99.7 % MG dye concentration. The catalyst showed excellent stability after 4 reuse cycles

TiO2 thin films with carbon quantum dots (C-QDs/TiO2)

10, 5 and 4 mg L−1 of PNP, MB and RhB, respectively.
10 mL reaction
CQDs source: citric acid and urea
Four UV-A lamps (λ = 350–390 nm, light intensity = 0.5 mW cm−1)

CQDs act supressing the TiO2 photogenerated charge carrier recombination and changing the mechanisms of degradation. h
+ is the main species in the degradation of MB and RhB, while the photoinduced e
– are the main species in the reduction of PNP

87.53 % of PNP was reduced to 4-amino phenol in 120 min. In addition, 79.78 % and 81.49 % degradation rate of RhB and MB was achieved, respectively. The films yielded good results even after 5 reuse cycles
CQDs/Cu2O
TC antibiotic

1.11 g L−1 of catalyst
10 mg L−1 of TC
90 mL reaction
CQDs source:
Corn Stover
Visible Xe lamp

S-Scheme heterojunction
CQDs enhances the absorption of visible light and reduce the recombination of charge carriers generated in the CB and VB of Cu2O. O2
•
− and HO• radicals are generated when the photoinduced e
– of the CB and the h
+ of the VB react with O2 and H2O, respectively
3 wt% CQDs/Cu2O degraded 92.49 % of TC within 100 min. The composite was reused in 4 consecutive reuse cycles
ZnO/CQDs nanoflawer
MB dye

0.05 g L−1 of catalyst
10 mg L−1 of MB
50 mL of reaction
CQDs source: lime juice (Citrus aurantifolia)
Solar light simulator, (λ = 400–1100 nm)

Due to the up-conversion properties of CQD, the incident visible light is absorbed and converted to a shorter wavelength light that is absorbed by ZnO, whose promoted e
– in the CB and their h
+ in the VB, react with O2 and H2O producing O2
•
− and HO• radicals, respectively
The composite degraded 91 % of MB within 30 min of light exposure. The catalyst successfully degraded MB until the 4th reuse cycle

Methylene Blue (MB) dye

0.2 g L−1 of catalyst
10 mL of reaction
400 W visible sodium lamp (λ max = 400–900 nm)
It is stated that the high charge carries recombination rate of Cu2O is compensated by the high specific surface area of the composite, but the generation of free radicals is not explored
The composite degraded 94 % of MB after 150 min of irradiation. The reduction in photocatalytic degradation rate after 6 reuse cycles was less than 3.5 %
WO3 /CNT. 10 wt% was the optimal proportion of CNT
Tetracycline (TC) antibiotic and pharmaceutical wastewaters (PWWs)

0.7 g L−1 of catalyst
60 mg L−1 of TC
300 mL reaction pH = 9
Ultrasounds 250 W m−2

Three 40W visible lamps
120 W m−2 intensity

In addition to light irradiation, ultrasounds generate photo-induced charge carriers and even dissociates water and generates O2
•
−, HO• and HO2 • radical species. the photoinduced e
– in the CB of WO3 are storage in the surface of CNT reacting with O2, while the h
+ of the VB are attacked by water

Complete degradation was achieved in 60 min. Catalyst was reused 6 times with only 8.8 % efficiency reduction.
83.7 and 90.6 % of total organic carbon (TOC) and chemical oxygen demand (COD), respectively, were achieved when PWWs were treated
N,Fe co-doped TiO2 @SWCNT (NFeTFC)
Sulfathiazole (SUTZ) antimicrobial and PWWs

0.7 g L−1 of catalyst
40 mg L−1 of SUTZ
200 mL reaction
Ultrasounds 250 W m−2

Three 40W visible lamps
150 W m−2 intensity

Fe doping leads to the formation of sub-conduction band under CB of TiO2 acting as an e
– /h
+ trapping agent, also N-doping narrows the bandgap. SWCNT prevents e
– /h
+ recombination acting as an e
– sink
Catalyst was reused 7 times. 79 and 88 % of TOC and COD, respectively, were achieved when PWWs were treated
TiO2 nanoparticles and CNTs loaded on poly(methyl methacrylate) nanofibers (PMMA)
MB, Rhodamine B (RhB), Sulforhodamine B (SRB) and methyl Orange (MOr) dyes

0.1–1 g L−1 of catalyst
10 mg L−1 of pollutants
100 mL reaction
32 W UV mercury lamp (λ max = 254 nm)

Since the potential of the CB of TiO2 is higher than that of CNTs, the latter act as an e
– sink suppressing charge recombination. Afterwards, the e
– in the CNTS are transferred to O2 producing O2
•
− radicals, while the h
+ in TiO2 produce HO• radicals from H2O

Using 0.2 g L of the catalyst and after 50 min of UV irradiation, degradation rates of 100 %, 78.1 %, 70.9 % and 98.8 % were achieved for MB, RhB, SRB and MOr, respectively.
Composites were used in 10 consecutive reuse cycles

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: WWEM-GEET-GC-2024 published in Environmental Research.

head.ce:title: Carbon-based metal oxide nanocomposites for water treatment by photocatalytic processes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic and Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Experimental Science, University of Jaén, Jaén, 23071, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic and Organic Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic and Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Experimental Science, University of Jaen, Jaen, 23071, Spain.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Farmacy, University of Granada, Granada, 18011, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, Faculty of Farmacy, University of Granada, Granada, 18011, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing contamination of water by emerging contaminants and the need for more efficient and sustainable treatment methods have prompted the exploration of advanced materials and technologies, with a particular focus on photocatalysis. Carbon-based metal oxide nanocomposites are a promising solution for the treatment of polluted water. This paper aims to review the current state of research on the application of these nanocomposites as photocatalysts for complete water treatment, describing breakthroughs in contaminant removal from 2019 through 2024 and milestones in water disinfection from 2016 through 2024. It includes discussion on the utilization of nanocomposites of Metal Oxides (MOs) with carbon materials to improve photocatalytic efficiency and addresses the advantages and drawbacks of these materials, including electron-hole recombination and agglomeration. The review focuses on the photocatalytic mechanisms of these nanocomposites and highlights the importance of heterostructures formed between metal oxides and carbon materials (e.g., graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots), which enhance light absorption and hydroxyl radical generation, thereby increasing the efficiency of pollutant degradation and water disinfection. The review describes the properties of different MOs (n-type and p-type), exploring synergies between MOs and carbon materials and discussing the benefits and challenges of their application in wastewater treatment and pathogen inactivation. The review ends with a scientometric analysis of research trends in this field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The steady progress of industrial processes and the rapid growth of the world population have generated an exponential increase in the discharge of chemical pollutants into the aquatic environment, contributing to a serious deterioration in water quality (Akhtar et al., 2021). This has driven efforts to enhance and optimize water treatments, improving the rate of pollutant removal with the lowest possible consumption of energy and chemicals. However, many contaminants are not easily removed using conventional techniques (i.e., membrane separation, chemical oxidation, ion exchange, biological methods, activated carbon adsorption, or ozonation). These include so-called emerging contaminants (ECs), which are currently unregulated and relatively unknown but can have negative health effects (Esplugas et al., 2007). Research is ongoing on the development of methods and materials to remove ECs before their discharge into aqueous systems (Niu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019).

Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) are highly efficient technologies to eliminate a wide variety of chemical compounds from water. They generate strong oxidant species that are highly effective to oxidize numerous ECs present in water at trace concentrations. AOPs are applied to achieve total mineralization of organic pollutants via oxidation reactions to stabilize inorganic compounds such as carbon dioxide (CO2), water (H2O), or mineral acids (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2020). The oxidants most frequently used for this purpose have been ozone, hydrogen peroxide, and chlorine, among others, and these agents have proven less effective to degrade ECs when used individually (Hübner et al., 2024) than when they are combined with each other (e.g. O3/H2O2) or with sources of irradiation (UV/H2O2, UV/K2S2O2, etc.) (Rizzo et al., 2019). Therefore, catalytic materials have been incorporated into these systems to obtain a more efficient production of oxidant radicals. Heterogeneous photocatalysis is a photochemical process that has been widely investigated in water treatment systems (Rizzo et al., 2019). However, photocatalysts have been associated with certain drawbacks, including their difficult reutilization, the low percentage absorption of solar irradiation, and the frequent recombination of electron (e
– )/positive hole (h
+ ) pairs. Recent studies have focused on: the preparation of semiconductors with heterojunctions; doping with metal oxide (MO) or noble metals; and the incorporation of cocatalysts to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of a single photocatalyst (Di Paola et al., 2012; Porcu et al., 2022). Photocatalytic nanocomposites have improved the performance of MO nanoparticles (NPs) as potential semiconductors in water treatments (Hoffmann et al., 1995; Pattnaik et al., 2023; Shanaah et al., 2023) thanks to their exceptional thermal and chemical stability, biocompatibility, light absorption properties, and electric charge transfer characteristics (e
– and h
+ ); in addition, these nanomaterials can be reutilized over multiple water treatment cycles (Pereira et al., 2022). Hence, MO can act as a photocatalyst when able to generate the relocation of electrons after absorption of light at an intensity not lower than the bandgap value of the MO semiconductor. The resulting photogenerated charges can be transferred to other molecules on the photocatalyst surface. The e
– transferred to the conduction band (CB) can react with an acceptor species if its redox potential is below the CB of the MO photocatalyst. In the same way, the positive h
+ can oxidize an e
– donor molecule if its redox potential is above the valence band (VB) of the MO semiconductor. Thus, the adsorbed hydrated hydroxyl groups (–OH) contribute to the production of oxidant species, while the adsorbed oxygen (O2) can be reduced to the superoxide radical (O2 •–). Generation of both radical oxidant species and direct oxidation by reaction with positive holes are the usual pathways for the degradation of contaminants (Pereira et al., 2022; Zhu and Zhou, 2019) and the inactivation of pathogenic microorganisms in disinfection processes.

Catalytic reactivity is enhanced by the small size and comparatively large surface area of nanometric MO semiconductors (Hossain et al., 2023; Hristovski et al., 2007) because these properties produce a higher density of active sites, facilitating interaction with diverse chemical species (Li et al., 2006; Phivilay et al., 2013). Despite advances in photocatalysis research, its application faces some challenges. Thus, in unmodified semiconductors, electrons generated by light absorption quickly recombine with holes in the VB, which reduces the availability of photo-excited carriers and consequently the overall efficiency of the process. MOs at nanometer level are susceptible to agglomeration, mainly by weak van der Waals bonds, and/or the formation of aggregates by strong chemical bonds, and/or electrostatic and steric interactions due to their large surface area (Jiang et al., 2009; Özcan-Taşkin et al., 2009; Pradeep and Anshup, 2009). These drawbacks could therefore invalidate the benefits offered by their nanoscale size.

Furthermore, MO can form nanocomposites with other MOs or with functional materials to obtain a larger surface area, lower e
– /h
+ recombination, and higher photocatalytic performance in comparison to pure MO (Jamjoum et al., 2021; Pereira et al., 2022). Various techniques have been developed for this purpose, coupling MO with a wide range of carbon materials. Heterojunction structures created by the union of two semiconductors provide an efficient pathway for extending the photo-reactive range and maintaining the advantages of the nanometric size. The two semiconductors must have distinct Fermi energy levels in order to create an intrinsic electric field at the heterojunction interface, achieving the spontaneous migration of charge carriers from the semiconductor with higher Fermi energy to that with lower Fermi energy. A major increase in photocatalytic efficiency is produced by the presence of carbon materials in the nanocomposite structure. This can be explained by a combination of different effects, involving structural strategies and simultaneous compositional optimizations. These include: a decrease in e
– /h
+ recombinations, given that the carbon-carbon (C–C) bond can store electrons (sp3 e
– bond and π bond); an improvement in carrier allocation due to e
– mobility throughout graphene (GR) layers via the π-system formed by sp2 hybridization; and an increase in catalytic active sites on the larger surface area (Boehm, 2012). In addition, the C–O–Metal bond may reduce the semiconductor bandgap and increase the range of visible irradiation captured by the photocatalyst (Khan, 2021; Yang et al., 2023). These properties offer an exponential advance in the implementation of this approach in solar-driven water treatment (Goh and Ismail, 2021; Raza et al., 2022; Son et al., 2021).

Various review articles have been published on advances in carbon-based photocatalysts over the past five years. However, they have frequently lacked depth in two key areas: the heterojunction properties obtained in carbon materials with metal oxide nanoparticles (MO-NPs) and the complex reaction mechanisms that govern photocatalytic processes in these nanocomposites (Lopes et al., 2021; Oluwole et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2024)

Moreover, in regard to the removal of chemical pollutants from water, nanocomposites described in reviews have only incorporated certain specific carbon allotropes for photocatalytic treatment, most frequently basing nanocomposites on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Gangu et al., 2019; Sendão et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2019a), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (Kumar et al., 2017; Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2021; Selvaraj et al., 2020), or GR as a hot-topic material (Dutta et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2020). A further challenge being addressed by AOPs is to disinfect water without oxidant chemical additives and their byproducts. Recent research on carbon-based nanocomposite photocatalysts for bacteria inactivation has mainly focused on GR, which is the only material included in three review articles on water disinfection (Gao et al., 2010; Kusiak-Nejman and Morawski, 2019; Yin et al., 2023).

The aim of this review is to address these critical gaps in knowledge by in-depth analysis of the reaction mechanisms associated with different heterostructures formed by MO-NPs in carbon-based nanomaterials, elucidating the synergistic phenomena involved in contaminant degradation and water disinfection processes. Heterojunctions formed in these nanocomposites are rigorously examined, studying Fermi levels and electronic band structures to establish their precise role in photocatalysis and underlying reaction mechanisms. The review offers a comprehensive overview of the application of these nanocomposites in pollutant removal and in water disinfection and highlights their potential for offering integrated solutions to multiple water quality challenges. A central theme is the capacity to harness visible light, which has profound implications for both wastewater treatment and pathogen inactivation.

This review updates researchers on the current state of carbon-based MO nanocomposites as photocatalysts for water remediation. It explores synergies derived from the composition of nanocomposites, determines the principal properties that influence its photocatalytic performance, describes applications in holistic water treatments, and highlights trends, challenges, and future research perspectives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocomposite photocatalysts based on MOs and carbon materials

ce:section.ce:para:
Various mechanisms have been suggested for the photocatalysis process in nanocomposite materials formed by coupling two semiconductors, as in the case of MO with carbon materials (Fig. 1 ). The interface between two semiconductors with different band structures is known as a heterojunction and can result in new band configurations that create multiple radiation responses (Bai et al., 2022; Gao et al., 2010; Yin et al., 2023). Photogenerated charge carrier separation is the main aim of heterojunction creation, because it reduces the recombination of e
– /h
+ pairs, which is a major limitation on photocatalytic efficiency. Separated electrons and holes can then participate in redox reactions with molecules adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface. Heterojunctions produce a higher concentration of charge carriers available for redox reactions, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity. There are three types of basic heterojunctions, classified according to the mobility of photogenerated charges (Fig. 1) (Marschall, 2014; Moniz et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2012).

Type-I heterojunctions have a straddling gap, with the CB of one semiconductor above the CB of the other and the VB of the former below the VB of the latter. Upon irradiation therefore, negative and positive photogenerated charges are gathered at the CB and VB levels of the second semiconductor, respectively. The consequent accumulation of both electrons and positive charges on the same semiconductor generates a redox reaction on the semiconductor with lower redox potential, resulting in a higher recombination rate of photogenerated e
– /h
+ pairs and an important reduction in the redox capacity of type-I heterojunction photocatalysts. By contrast, type-II heterojunctions have a staggered gap, which offers optimal band positions for charge carrier separation and extends the lifetime of photogenerated carriers. This is because the CB and VB of the first semiconductor are both higher with respect to the second semiconductor. For this reason, photogenerated electrons migrate from the first to the second semiconductor, while the corresponding positive holes migrate from the second semiconductor to the first. However, when Fermi energy levels differ between the semiconductors, acting as n-type and p-type materials, an electric field forms as a surface p-n heterojunction. N-type and p-type materials are semiconductors with an excess of valence electrons or holes, respectively. In type-II heterojunctions, electrons migrate to the CB of the n-type semiconductor and holes to the VB of the p-type semiconductor (Liu et al., 2016a; Sabzehparvar et al., 2021). Finally, type-III heterojunctions have a broken gap and may be considered as a type-II variant with marked differences in VB and CB positions between the semiconductors, resulting in such an extreme staggered gap that bandgaps do not overlap, and a greater driving force is needed for charge transfer. For this reason, type-III heterojunctions do not feature the e
– and h
+ disconnection between semiconductors and are not appropriate for separating photogenerated charge pairs. The most effective photocatalysts have a type-II heterojunction due to the capacity of this structure to spatially separate charges, its broad spectral absorption, and its fast mass transfer. Nanostructures usually have type-II heterojunctions because their optimal charge carrier separation enhances the photocatalytic activity of nanocomposites; however, the redox potential of transferred carriers is reduced because the reactions take place in the CB and VB positions with lower redox potential energy.

There are also three main types of photogenerated charge transfer: the Z, S, and Schottky schemes. Simulating natural photosynthesis, the Z-scheme is an efficient mechanism with solar irradiation harvesting because it overcomes the drawbacks of type-I and type-II heterojunctions by having a high redox capacity, maintaining effective separation of the photo-assisted charge carrier (Li et al., 2016a). Fermi levels of p-type and n-type semiconductors are close to their VB and CB, respectively. Photoinduced electrons migrate from the less negative CB of the n-type semiconductor and recombine with photogenerated holes from the less positive VB of the p-type semiconductor. In contrast to type II heterojunctions, the Z-Scheme generates a strong Coulomb electrostatic repulsive force that does not permit transfer of electrons in the most negative CB to the CB of the second semiconductor heterojunction (Xu et al., 2018). Likewise, a high redox capacity is maintained due to the accumulation of electrons on the CB of the second semiconductor and of positive holes on the VB of the first. Hence, the system is assisted by electrostatic interactions due to the energy bands of the e
– donor and acceptor semiconductor, with the nanosized carbon material usually acting as e
– acceptor (Li et al., 2016a; Lu et al., 2019). S-scheme heterojunctions combine the charge-diffusion pathway of the Z-scheme with the band structure of type-II heterojunctions; they are characterized by a “step-type” transfer of photogenerated electrons, usually between n-type semiconductors (Li et al., 2024). In this new model, photoinduced electrons migrate from the less negative CB of the first semiconductor and recombine with photogenerated holes from the less positive VB of the other semiconductor until the Fermi energy level is offset. The diffusion of electrons creates an internal electric field with a band that bends until the Fermi energy aligns to equilibrium, resulting in the recombination of useless electrons and corresponding holes and their elimination at the interface. In summary, the S-scheme heterojunction is the most efficient mechanism because it inhibits recombination of the most useful electrons and holes (Deng et al., 2023).

The electric field can be enhanced at the interface of semiconductors by the deposition of metal NPs due to surface plasmon resonance at the contact interface (Li et al., 2012). This semiconductor–metal NP heterojunction creates the so-called Schottky heterojunction, in which photogenerated electrons are captured by metal NPs that have a lower Fermi level with respect to the semiconductor CB. Photoexcited e
– storage in the semiconductor prevents their recombination with positive holes, thereby avoiding charge carrier recombination (Xue et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2019a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General consideration of the most common MO semiconductors in photocatalytic systems
ce:section.ce:para: A wide variety of MO-NPs are photoactive due to their capacities for electronic structure organization, light absorption, and charge transport (Bai et al., 2022; Khan et al., 2019; Theerthagiri et al., 2018). Particular attention has been paid to MO-NPs as n-type or p-type semiconductors as a promising strategy for photoinduced charge separation and light harvesting.

N-type semiconductors are e
– donors due to their CB energy level. The Fermi level is closer to the CB than the VB, and charges relocate from lower to higher energy levels (Yang, 2021). N-type semiconductors have the highest e
– density in the composite and are usually doped with group V elements of the periodic system. The photocatalytic MOs most frequently used in n-type semiconductors are titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), tungsten oxide (WO3), iron oxide (Fe2O3), tin oxides (SnO, and SnO2).

Titanium dioxide. Since its application for the photocatalytic degradation of cyanide in 1977 (Hair, 1967) TiO2 has become the main photocatalytic material used in the setting of environmental pollution because it is well-known, low-cost, and non-toxic, with high chemical stability and striking resistance to corrosion (Lan et al., 2013). TiO2 is present in nature as three polymorphic phases: rutile, anatase, and brookite (Carp et al., 2004), and only brookite is non-photoactive (Lan et al., 2013). Bandgap values for rutile and anatase are 3.0 and 3.2 eV, respectively, and UV irradiation is necessary for photocatalysis with TiO2 materials, limiting its application under solar irradiation.

Zinc oxide. ZnO is also low-cost, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly. It is a strong competitor for conventional TiO2 due to its excellent e
– mobility and greater variety of structures and point defects (due to oxygen vacancies), favoring the generation of more oxidant radicals (Ong et al., 2018; Qamar et al., 2021). ZnO NPs have been used in composites with TiO2 materials to widen light absorption towards the visible spectra and enhance the photocatalytic activity (Li et al., 2014; Moradi et al., 2016).

Despite their relatively high bandgap value (3.37 eV), ZnO NPs have been used under solar irradiation. They can produce e
– /h
+ pairs under UV irradiation and exhibit an extended solar response under the visible light spectrum (Nguyen et al., 2020).

Tungsten oxide. WO3 is an inexpensive and abundant semiconductor with low toxicity, strong photo-corrosion resistance, and chemical stability. It is characterized by a rapid recombination of photogenerated charges but shows a strong response to solar irradiation due to its limited bandgap (2.4–2.6 eV) (Tahir et al., 2017). WO3 differs from other n-type semiconductors in the high oxidizing capacity of the positive holes located in the VB due to its energy value, EVB = + 3.1–3.2 VNHE (Kim et al., 2010, 2015). Despite the promising photocatalytic properties of WO3, its photocatalytic efficiency is limited by its low CB energy value (ECB = + 0.3–0.5 VNHE), which is more positive than the energy potential of E0 (O2/O2 •−) = – 0.33 VNHE (Kim et al., 2010, 2015). Hence, WO3 NPs cannot provide sufficient potential for O2 reduction, and the hydroxyl radical (HO•) is the primary reactive species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants in water (Ikram et al., 2021).

Iron oxides. Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 are the most common iron oxides in nature, and their crystal phases are hematite (α-Fe2O3), maghemite (γ-Fe2O3), and magnetite (Fe3O4). The magnetic properties of γ-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 have led to their application in water treatments, frequently combined with other semiconductors to prepare composites for the efficient and simple removal of suspended photocatalysts from water by magnetic separation, with no need for additional separation processes (Singh et al., 2019; Su, 2017). On the other hand, α-Fe2O3, which can be synthesized inexpensively, can be an efficient standalone photocatalyst due to its bandgap energy (between 2.0 and 2.25 eV) and high stability in aqueous solution (Akhavan and Azimirad, 2009; Velempini et al., 2021). Nevertheless, α-Fe2O3 NPs are among the most frequently coupled semiconductors in nanocomposites for the purpose of extending the visible absorption range of other semiconductors (Velempini et al., 2021). As in the case of WO3, its high VB energy value (EVB = +2.52 eV) means that oxidation is the dominant degradation pathway for organic pollutants in water when Fe2O3 is the photocatalyst. Hence, the O2 reduction reaction to generate O2 •– radicals (ECB = +0.27eV) becomes unfavorable (Jia et al., 2020).

Tin oxides. Photocatalysts based on tin materials are emerging as a new group of interest due to their cost-effective photocatalytic capacity, negligible toxicity, chemical stability, corrosion resistance, and strong oxidation potential. Given their wide bandgap (Eg [SnO2] = + 3.6 eV; Eg [SnO] = + 2.5–3.0 eV), tin oxides mainly absorb in the UV region (Ahmad et al., 2023; Batzill and Diebold, 2005). SnO2 is the most widely used phase of tin oxides because it has higher stability compared with stannous oxide (SnO). In fact, the properties of SnO2 are very similar to those of TiO2 but it also shares the same drawbacks as a photocatalyst. Nevertheless, SnO2 performs poorly in water decontamination because its photoactivity efficiency is reduced by a significant recombination of photoinduced e
– /h
+ pairs (Ahmad et al., 2023; Yadav and Ahmaruzzaman, 2023). Interestingly, the dual valency in tin oxide semiconductors facilitates the application of a SnO/SnO2 combination as a p-n heterojunction-based photocatalyst in nanocomposites. This may pose a solution to the high e
– /h
+ recombination rate, because Sn2+ functions as an e
– trap and thereby increases photocatalytic activity (Phuong et al., 2021; Santhi et al., 2016).

P-type semiconductors act as an e
– acceptor because the Fermi level is closer to the VB than CB energy level (Yang, 2021). P-type semiconductors have a higher h
+ density in the composite, which is usually doped with group III elements in the periodic system. The photoactive MOs most often used as p-type semiconductors are copper oxide (Cu2O, CuO), nickel oxide (NiO), and bismuth (III) oxide (Bi2O3).

Copper oxides . Cu2O and CuO are the most widely applied p-type semiconductors for photocatalytic systems due to their favorable bandgap values and the availability of their precursors. Bandgap values of copper oxides are 1.9–2.0 eV for cuprous oxide (Cu2O) and 1.2–1.7 eV for cupric oxide (CuO) (Sabzehei et al., 2020). Both are among the small number of semiconductors with a reduced bandgap that can operate under UV and visible light, presenting a high absorption coefficient across the solar wavelength range. Their photocatalytic efficiencies follow different pathways due to their singular CB and VB values. Cu2O has a potent reduction pathway because its ECB = – 1.45 VNHE is more negative than the redox potential of E0 (O2/O2
•
−) = −0.33 VNHE, while its oxidizing capacity is non-existent, despite being a semiconductor. This is due to its unusual less positive EVB value of +1.1–1.2 VNHE, so that the water oxidation reaction is thermodynamically inviable (E0 [H2O/HO • ] = +2.27 VNHE) (Bai et al., 2022). In contrast, the more positive CB and VB values in CuO semiconductors only allow a high oxidation route, due to the high oxidizing capacity of the positive holes at its VB energy level (EVB = +2.1 VNHE) (Bai et al., 2022). Furthermore, when CuO and Cu2O are jointly applied, the absorption radiation spectra range from the visible region to near-infrared, as a synergic effect (Yang et al., 2016). Hence, thanks to their distinctive optical characteristics, copper oxides therefore offer numerous opportunities for the development of nanocomposite, while the low cost of these semiconductors and their availability from readily available precursors facilitates the sustainable larger-scale production of photocatalytic materials (Bashiri Rezaie et al., 2018; Visibile et al., 2020). However, photooxidation of Cu2O to CuO destroys the morphology of Cu2O, and this serious photo-corrosion problem limits its photocatalytic application (Ansari et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2016; Toe et al., 2018). Hence, significant improvements in the properties of copper oxide photocatalysts have been proposed to lengthen their lifetime, designing protective overlayer heterojunctions that remove electrons from Cu2O in accordance with the redox cascade principle (Kwon et al., 2015; Mohammed et al., 2021; Yadav and Ahmaruzzaman, 2023).

Nickel oxide. NiO has been assigned a relatively high bandgap value of 3.4–3.6 eV (Bai et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2019a; Mohammadijoo et al., 2014), limiting its application to UV-C radiation photocatalysis (Hayat et al., 2011). NiO-NPs have high chemical stability, a low dielectric constant, and high catalytic activity, and they exhibit antibacterial behavior (Gondal et al., 2011; Shanaj et al., 2016). Given its low capacity to harvest visible light, NiO is commonly used to boost the photocatalytic efficiency of other oxide semiconductors towards the visible region with a Z-scheme heterojunction. Photocatalytic activity is also enhanced by the increased density of active centers and surface defects in these nanocomposite semiconductors (Asaithambi et al., 2019; Guan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019). However, one advantage of NiO over other MO semiconductors is its good anti-corrosion property, and its utilization as co-catalyst in nanocomposites can increase the stability of semiconductors (Ethiraj et al., 2020).

Bismuth (III) oxide. Bi2O3, which has a bandgap value of 2.8 eV and is non-toxic, has attracted increasing interest as a photocatalyst (Chen et al., 2010; Wu et al., 2011; Xian et al., 2021). It has six different polymorph phases. β-Bi2O3 is the phase most widely used as a photocatalyst, despite having the lowest bandgap value (2.48 eV) (Muruganandham et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2011), thanks to its high stability and the strong photocatalytic oxidation power of its superior positive VB energy (EVB = +2.9 VNHE; ECB = −0.07 VNHE) (Leontie et al., 2001; Xie et al., 2020). However, its practical application is hampered by the rapid recombination of photogenerated charges and weak absorption of visible irradiation. For this reason, it is more widely applied as a co-catalyst in heterojunction composites than as a single oxide semiconductor (Hao et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2023). Given the particular optical properties of Bi2O3, its participation in nanocomposites usually results in a Z-scheme heterojunction, which improves their photocatalytic performance by boosting charge transfer and visible spectrum capabilities (Lan et al., 2020; Pang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect of carbon nanomaterials in composites based on MO semiconductors
ce:section.ce:para:
The striking photocatalytic activity of transition MOs is well documented. However, as commented above, certain unfavorable properties have delayed their widespread application in real-scale water treatments. They include: the wide bandgap of MO, resulting in poor photoactivity under solar irradiation; the rapid recombination of charges; chemical instability, in some cases; and the formation of NP aggregates that block catalytic active sites. One of the most widely researched methods to overcome these photocatalytic challenges is to form heterojunctions with two or more semiconductors by nanocomposite synthesis. Coupled MO-NPs reduce e
– /h
+ recombination by introducing charge carrier trapping sites and by narrowing the bandgap through the insertion of new energy levels, and both effects increase the photocatalytic activity, especially under visible light irradiation (Abebe et al., 2020; Lettieri et al., 2021; Theerthagiri et al., 2018). In comparison to other materials, however, MO-coupled photocatalysts are more expensive and are more prone to photo-corrosion (Danish et al., 2020; Toe et al., 2019). These issues have been addressed by utilizing non-metals as carbon-based materials for the development of photocatalysts with a photo-response in the visible light region. While carbon-based nanostructures have the same bonding configurations as macroscopic carbon materials, their properties and morphology result in a synergistic effect on photon collection and catalytic action (Cao and Yu, 2016; Khan et al., 2019). In this way, the higher photocatalytic yield of nanocomposites with carbon materials is associated with improvements in the specific surface area and in the array structure of the resulting semiconductor.

The best-known application of carbon materials is as a support to anchor MO-NPs. NP-based semiconductors can be distributed on the large surface area of carbon materials and immobilized by the reaction of developing NPs with defect sites and oxygen-containing groups. The presence of these well-distributed nucleation sites favors the good homogeneous dispersion and stabilization of NP semiconductors, avoiding agglomeration during their application (Anil Kumar et al., 2023; Gerber and Serp, 2020). Carbon materials have also demonstrated unparallel hardness in acidic and basic media, allowing potential regulation of their porosity and surface chemistry. The stability of carbon materials means they remain inert during the doping process in MO-NP processes, preserving their structure and properties (Lopes et al., 2021; Yi et al., 2018).

Furthermore, carbon-based composite photocatalysts offer major benefits for water decontamination and disinfection, highlighting their adsorption capacity for pollutant removal. Carbon-based nanocomposites can first gather trace pollutants and microorganisms on their surface and then oxidize them in situ via photocatalytic reactions (Gusain et al., 2019; Qumar et al., 2022; Upadhyay et al., 2014). Their larger surface area also increases the accessibility of water, organic molecules, pathogens, or microorganisms to active sites in the nanocomposite, accelerating the redox reaction by increasing the production of radical species (Madima et al., 2020).

At the same time, the presence of carbon structures in the nanocomposite produces variations in the electronic properties of pristine MO semiconductors, addressing the drawbacks of a wide bandgap and rapid photogenerated charge recombination. Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) can achieve these improvements by acting as an e
– sink, which is facilitated by their high charge mobility and e
– storage capability within the sp3 and π bonds of their C-C structure. This mechanism effectively prevents the recombination of charge carriers. After the absorption of irradiation, electrons promoted from the VB of the MO can be injected into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of carbon materials. This is because the CB energies of the coupled MO are usually more negative than the Fermi energy values of CNMs. Next, electrons are transported across the carbon material surface through the π-system formed by sp2 hybridization, ensuring an efficient charge separation in the photocatalytic nanocomposite (He et al., 2021b; Yadav and Ahmaruzzaman, 2023). The newly created C–O–Metal bonds between MO and CNMs can also reduce the bandgap, extending the solar spectrum absorbed by the nanocomposite. These chemical bonds result in new energy levels for the transfer of photogenerated electrons, developing the heterojunctions mechanisms described above (section 2.1).

Carbon materials can also be used as stand-alone photocatalysts in aqueous systems, producing oxidant agents that can be used to remove chemical and microbiological pollutants (Velo-Gala et al., 2013, 2017). The NP size of carbon materials induces a more negative LUMO position than the O2 reduction potential, favoring both oxidant and reductive pathways for oxidant radical production. The carbon materials most frequently applied as photocatalysts are quantum dots, fullerenes, nanotubes, and GR, thanks to their semiconductor properties and NP size (Ahmed et al., 2024; He et al., 2021a). Another interesting feature of CNMs is that they can display photosensitizer-like behavior when incident photon energy is not sufficient to promote e
– transition to LUMO level. The presence of C=C and C–O bonds in carbon materials produces π-π∗ and n-π∗ electronic transitions, respectively, resulting in a strong absorption of UV irradiation that extends into the near-visible range. If the LUMO of the CNM has a more negative potential than the CB of a semiconductor incapable of visible light excitation, photoexcited electrons within the carbon material can be injected into the semiconductor's CB (Buchalska et al., 2013; Sohail et al., 2024).

Quantum dots, fullerenes, nanotubes, and GR are carbon-based nanostructures with good catalytic effectiveness. Although beyond the scope of this review, the catalytic properties of foams and carbon sheaths, among other less widely applied carbon-based nanostructures, have been enhanced by integration with MOs, exhibiting a good photocatalytic performance in contaminant removal under visible light irradiation (Li et al., 2020b; Lu et al., 2020; Mohamed et al., 2023).

Fig. 2 depicts the photocatalytic performance of different catalysts against tetracycline (TC) and methylene blue (MB), showing that Fe2O3@Ag−ZnO@C Z-scheme heterostructures with carbon sheaths have the highest adsorption efficiency and that the addition of carbon sheaths increases the contaminant degradation rate.
The role as photocatalyst of each CNM (quantum dots, fullerenes, nanotubes, and GR) in MO-NP composites is critically discussed below.

Carbon Quantum Dots. CQDs are quasi-spherical carbon NPs less than 10 nm in size (Liu et al., 2016b). CQDs in nanocomposites are donors and acceptors for e
– transfer (Gao et al., 2022; Kc et al., 2015), and increase the contact area (Cao and Yu, 2016). Thus, the larger surface area of CQD/Bi5O7I composites was reported to improve pollutant removal by providing more adsorption and reaction sites for contaminants (Chen et al., 2018). Importantly, the optical properties of CQDs as quantum dots combine with their electrical properties as sp2 carbon-based materials (Liu et al., 2016b; Tuerhong et al., 2017). CQDs have therefore been classified as a new type of photosensitizer characterized by UV–Vis irradiation absorption, fluorescence emission, and up-conversion effects (Hutton et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2014). When CQDs are excited by external irradiation, the initial energy state jumps to an excited state, which results in deactivation of emitted photons and a return to their initial state, thereby giving rise to fluorescence. In CQDs, the intensity of this fluorescence depends not only on the excitation wavelength but also on the particle size and surface defects (Boruah et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2015). Furthermore, CQDs exhibit luminescence properties, transforming long wavelength radiation into high-energy radiation that can be applied by photocatalysts to produce positive holes and electrons for photocatalytic reactions (Cao et al., 2007; Ding et al., 2016). Various authors have described a higher photocatalytic activity for the CQD/TiO2 composite than for pure TiO2, attributed to the production of more electrons and e
– /h
+ pairs under light irradiation, and the CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite is a good photocatalyst (Chen et al., 2016; Sendão et al., 2022). Other nanocomposites, such as ZnO/CQD and Bi2O3/CQD nanocomposites, also favor photocatalytic activity through the formation of photogenerated charges (Bozetine et al., 2016; Sharma et al., 2019b). Pan et al. (2016) found that the SnO2/CQD composite has excellent photocatalytic properties under visible light irradiation (Fig. 3 ) (Pan et al., 2016). Up-conversion luminescence results from simultaneous multiphoton absorption that has been related to graphite nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQDs (Ding et al., 2016; Li et al., 2021).

Fig. 3a presents the SEM image of the SnO2 nanotube surface, which shows a granular appearance that may favor adsorption of contaminant molecules onto the nanotubes and improve the contact area between them. Fig. 3b depicts the XRD spectra of SnO2 nanotubes at different concentrations of CQDs. No diffraction peaks are observed other than those corresponding to diffractions of typical crystal planes of tetragonal SnO2, indicating the highly crystalline SnO2 structure of the nanotube compounds. Fig. 3c-e exhibit TEM images that show the attachment of CQDs to the surface of SnO2 nanotubes. The lattice spacings in the HRTEM images are consistent with the (002) and (110) planes of CQDs and SnO2, respectively. Fig. 3f reveals the photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation of CQD-modified SnO2 nanotubes, showing the resulting improvement in their photocatalytic activity. Fig. 3g shows the intensification of absorption at higher CQD concentrations, in agreement with the increase in photocatalysis under visible light. Fig. 3h provides a schematic representation of the proposed system, in which CQDs/SnO2 nanotube composites are excited under visible light irradiation and generate photogenerated e
– /h
+ pairs, improving the photocatalytic activity, along with the transfer by the CQDs of photogenerated electrons.

Fullerenes. Fullerenes are characterized by their closed, hollow structure composed of sp2 carbon atoms in a framework of 12 pentagons and a calculable number of hexagons. This material is a carbon allotrope with a zero-dimensional (0D) nanocarbon structure. Among various well-established configurations of fullerenes (C60, C70, C76, C82, and C84), C60 is most widely investigated in photocatalysis due to its more efficient synthesis and its excellent property as e
– acceptor and transporter, capable of reversibly admitting six electrons (Li et al., 2016b; Pan et al., 2020). Fullerenes function as exceptional e
– acceptors and transporters in composite photocatalytic systems through their generation of the triplet excited state (Pan et al., 2020). A C60 composite with polyaniline showed the charge transfer interaction of complexes formed between C60 as e
– acceptor and polyaniline as e
– donor, with the formation of a triplet state (Mathew et al., 2024). Under the appropriate irradiation, C60 is excited to the singlet excited state with a lifetime of 40 μs. This excited state accepts up to six electrons from the CB of the semiconductor material in the nanocomposite, reducing the singlet excited state to the C60 radical anion. The resulting reactive interface generates a series of reactions between radical species, contaminants, and water (Panagiotou et al., 2010). In particular, there is an increased production of singlet oxygen molecules (1O2), whose oxidizing power can be applied for pollutant removal and pathogen inactivation (Krusic et al., 1991). Lee et al. (2010) reported that the high 1O2 generation yield of C60 and its derivatives supports their usefulness as a novel environmental photocatalyst for pollutant degradation and microbial inactivation (Lee et al., 2010). Fullerenes can also generate electrons and positive holes under light excitation due to their narrow bandgap (1.5–1.98 eV), which allows the transition of excited electrons from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the LUMO (Smazna et al., 2018) and enhances the photodegradation performance of the nanocomposites (Katsumata et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2011). Some authors have already reported these improvements in various C60 nanocomposites with MOs, including NiO/C60 and TiO2/C60 (Fig. 4 ) (Bashir et al., 2023; Regulska and Karpinska, 2014).

Fig. 4a-c depict SEM images of C60, C60/TiO2, and TiO2, respectively. In the prepared composite, smooth flakes corresponding to C60 can be observed alongside small particles, corresponding to TiO2. The C60 surface has been covered by TiO2 particles. Fig. 4d presents a TEM image of the C60/TiO2 (1:20) composite, revealing two well-differentiated zones. The spacings observed in these zones coincide with the (011) plane spacing of anatase (0.35 nm) and the spacing of face-centered cubic (fcc) crystalline C60 (0.81 nm). Fig. 4g plots ln (Co/Ct) against t for estimation of the pseudo first-order rate constant, kapp, and displays the half-life, t1/2, of photocatalytic MB degradation. The C60/TiO2 (1:20) composite had the highest photocatalytic activity, with a kapp of 0.0117 mol L−1 and t1/2 of 59 min. The increased photocatalytic activity is attributed to the presence of C60 in the prepared nanocomposites. Fig. 4h displays the UV–VIS diffuse-reflectance spectra of prepared C60/TiO2 composites and TiO2. C60/TiO2 has two absorption band edges, while TiO2 has only one. The presence of a second band edge in all C60/TiO2 composites indicates successful C60 doping. The difference in energy levels between the C60 LUMO orbital and TiO2 CB produces an e
– transfer from C60 to the CB. The e
– injected into the TiO2 leads to the creation of radicals responsible for the degradation of organic matter. These data suggest that C60/TiO2 composites can achieve greater photocatalytic activity under solar irradiation.

The photocatalytic and photosensitizer properties of fullerenes have been attributed to its narrow bandgap, and researchers have observed the transfer of photoexcited electrons from the fullerenes to the CB of semiconductors in the nanocomposite (Ito, 2017; Yamakoshi et al., 2003). In a study of the TiO2/C60 photocatalyst under UV light irradiation, Yao et al. found that the photogenerated electrons are excited from the VB of TiO2 to the CB, leaving holes in the VB. This composite has a high capacity for e
– retention, successfully suppressing charge carrier recombination and providing additional carriers to participate in the photocatalytic reaction (Yao et al., 2020). Nevertheless, despite these advantageous photocatalytic properties, there is debate about the increase in Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area reportedly obtained with the utilization of fullerenes to prepare nanocomposites (Cai et al., 2017; Ju et al., 2017).

Carbon nanotubes. CNTs are characterized by sp2 hybridization between C–C bonds. Their diameter is in the nanometer ranges and their length varies from microns to millimeters. The two main types are multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) and single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs), classified by the number of rolled GR layers that form their structure. The diameter of SWCNTs is 0.4–2 nm, and their length can span several micrometers, while the diameter of MWCNTs is 2–25 nm according to the number of GR layers which can be separated by a distance similar to that of GR in graphite (0.335–0.345 nm). The structure of MWCNTs is described by two models: 1) the Russian doll model, in which the wrapped layers form concentric tubes; and 2) the Parchment model, in which the structure of a single GR layer is analogous to that of a rolled parchment or newspaper. The morphology and properties of MWCNTs are like those of SWCNTs, but the former offer enhanced chemical and mechanical resistance, with high flexibility and thermal conductivity.

CNTs can increase the photocatalytic activity of nanocomposites in three possible ways: (i) avoiding photogenerated charge recombination in the MO by functioning as an e
– reservoir (Lee et al., 2012; Yadav et al., 2014); (ii) acting as a photosensitizer to produce positive holes and electrons that can form reactive radical species (Chen et al., 2010; Ma et al., 2014); and (iii) generating new bonds and energy levels through the formation of C–O–Metal bonds (Arora and Attri, 2020; Woan et al., 2009; Yan et al., 2015). The most significant photocatalytic benefits of CNTs in nanocomposites are mechanical, including: an increase in specific surface area (from 50 to 1300 m2 g−1), yielding abundant sites to anchor semiconducting MOs-NPs, and ensuring their wide dispersion on CNTs (Peigney et al., 2001; Serp and Castillejos, 2010). Research on the synthesis of CNTs with MOs such as ZnO and NiO has verified an increased percentage contaminant removal due to the large specific surface area and small size of the nanocomposites, making them attractive candidates for catalysis (Ahmadi Azqhandi et al., 2018; Al-Kadhi et al., 2022; Hussain et al., 2017).

Fig. 5 a-c shows SEM micrographs of pure ZnO, Ag@ZnO, and Ag@ZnO/MWCNT. Addition of MWCNTs improves the distribution of smaller particles on the surface of MWCNTs. The uniform distribution of NPs provides more reactive sites and results in a high adsorption performance of the nano-adsorbent. Fig. 5d displays the specific surface areas of Ag@ZnO and Ag@ZnO/MWCNT. The loading of nano-adsorbents with MWCNTs enlarges the specific surface area of the nanocomposite, which correlates with an increase in its sorption capacity during adsorption.

The particular mechanical properties of CNTs make them the filler CNM of choice for strengthening the composite structure (Coleman et al., 2006). They have very good electric conductivity (Bandgap = 0–2 eV) as a semiconductor (Fischer and Johnson, 1999; Odom et al., 2000) and can themselves act as photocatalysts (Liu et al., 2013). CNT nanocomposites with TiO2, WO3, among other MOs, have demonstrated excellent photocatalytic properties against organic contaminants (Hintsho et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2023). Furthermore, the surface properties of CNTs can be functionalized by introducing functional groups such as ketone, alcohol, phenol and acid moieties, or nitrogen, among others (Dubey et al., 2021; Mohd Nurazzi et al., 2021). These modifications can improve the adsorption of specific compounds, adjust specific metal-support interactions, and change their chemical behavior (e.g., hydrophilicity, pH, e
– donor/acceptor properties, etc.) (Arora and Attri, 2020; Kharlamova et al., 2022; Spitalsky et al., 2010; Sweetman et al., 2017). For example, CNT incorporation in the CNT/CuO nanocomposite provides a pathway for charge/e
– transfer and simultaneously decreases e
– /h
+ recombination, thereby increasing the concentration of reactive species that can degrade pollutants under direct sunlight. Its hybrid heterostructure further enhances the performance of CNT/CuO, which achieves greater solar irradiation-induced photodegradation in comparison to pure CuO and can be considered a useful photocatalyst (Shibu et al., 2023).

Graphene. The combination of MOs with GR or its derivatives graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) increases the photoactivity of the pristine materials in pollutant degradation (Fu et al., 2012; Kumar et al., 2023; Mishra and Acharya, 2021). Hu et al. observed a two-fold increase in photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation for WO3/GR composites compared with pristine WO3 (Fig. 6 ) (Hu et al., 2018). Likewise, Singh et al. (2024) reported an improved degradation of organic contaminants under solar irradiation for the NiO/rGO nanocomposite (Singh et al., 2024). Fig. 6a-c exhibit the TEM-EDS and HRTEM images of rGO, WO3, and WO3/GR compounds, and Fig. 6c reveals the hybrid nanoarchitecture of WO3 nanorods on the GR surface. Fig. 6d displays the XRD spectra of the samples, showing that the XRD patterns did not differ between WO3/GR compounds and WO3, with an overlap between the typical peaks of GR with the peaks of WO3, although the sharpness of the peaks indicates a very high degree of crystallinity for the synthesized WO3. Fig. 6e depicts MB photodegradation of MB under visible irradiation in the absence and presence of the photocatalysts. The photocatalytic efficiency of WO3/GR increased with higher GO content, improving the contaminant degradation rate. Fig. 6f exhibits the photocatalytic degradation mechanism involved, showing that irradiation of the material with a visible light source provides electrons in WO3 with sufficient energy to generate e
– /h
+ pairs and trigger reduction-oxidation reactions. GR acts as an e
– acceptor, improving the photocatalytic efficiency of WO3/GR nanocomposites by inhibiting the recombination rate of photogenerated e
– /h
+ pairs.

The unique sp2 hybrid carbon network of these carbon materials offers not only high quality 2-D photocatalyst support but also a circuit sheet with the useful capacity to connect electrical and redox properties. The presence of GR or derivatives in composites also augments their specific surface area, improving photon interactions with the materials and charge carrier transfers between water and catalyst (Kuvarega et al., 2021; Yang and Xu, 2013). Coupling MOs-NPs with GR and its derivatives avoids the corrosion and leachate of MOs, improving their usefulness in water treatment (Anwar et al., 2015). After exfoliation treatment and subsequent oxidation, GO and rGO reveal an electrochemical combination of both sp2 carbons bonded in conductive π states and sp3 oxidized carbons in nonconductive σ states (Sturala et al., 2018). The functional groups on the surface of GO and rGO and their heteroatomic irregularity and structural defects give these materials singular properties for their photocatalytic application. Furthermore, S. Zhang et al. (2020) reported that GO commonly has surface hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), keto (C=O) and epoxy (C–O–C) groups (Zhang et al., 2020b). These groups bestow hydrophilicity to both GO and rGO and act as active centers during photocatalysis (Jankovský et al., 2017; Sturala et al., 2018). GO is described as a high oxygen content monolayer sheet, with oxygen functional groups on 20–30 % of its surface (Ajala et al., 2022; Dave et al., 2016). The likelihood of MO-NP photo-corrosion depends on the charges stored in the catalysts. This problem can be minimized by using GO, which boosts the e
– transfer from catalysts to oxygenated groups, as an e
– acceptor, and enhances h
+ abstraction by hydrogen donor compounds (Chen et al., 2015; Ibrahim et al., 2021). Thus, the photo-corrosion rate was found to be far slower in GO/Ag3PO4 nanocomposites than in pure MO, providing greater structural stability during the photocatalytic process (Liang et al., 2012). In addition, oxygenated groups in GR-based nanocomposites can behave as nucleation centers, with reactions between the electronegative oxygen atoms and positively charged metal precursors, which facilitates the uniform distribution of MO-NP (Ibrahim et al., 2021; Stergiou et al., 2020).

Despite the important advantages of GO, its nonconductive σ states from sp3 oxidized carbons limit its application in electrical and thermal transport (Ajala et al., 2022; Dave et al., 2016). rGO addresses this limitation by reducing the oxygen content and regulating the C/O ratio, and the increase in conductive π states of the bonded carbons enhances its electrochemical activity. In addition, rGO is an active material with GR layers and a high density of active sites and defects that endow it with photocatalytic properties (Kavitha, 2022; Mohan et al., 2018). Modafferi et al. studied the electrochemical performance of the rGO/Fe2O3 nanocomposite and described a good reaction rate and material stability (Modafferi et al., 2019).

It has also been observed that GO/rGO hybrid materials contribute their own synergic properties to the nanocomposite (Modafferi et al., 2019). Jiang et al. (2013) showed that the performance of a GO photocatalyst can be optimized by changing the relative ratio of the surface epoxy (–O–) and –OH groups, supporting the joint incorporation of both GR derivatives in photocatalyst material (Jiang et al., 2013).

Wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) worldwide employ similar technologies to remove solids, eliminate microorganisms, degrade organic pollutants, and establish a correct proportion of O2. The aim is to ensure that treated water can be safely discharged into the natural environment, meets quality standards for industrial and agricultural applications, and/or is suitable for human consumption. However, conventional WWTP methods have demonstrated limited effectiveness to remove recalcitrant chemical contaminants of emerging concern, and new approaches are required (Deblonde et al., 2011; Tran et al., 2018). AOPs are considered the best tertiary treatment of water for eliminating recalcitrant and toxic organic pollutants, microbes, viruses, and emerging threats (e.g., microplastics) through the generation of extremely reactive oxygen species (ROS) to degrade or even completely mineralize organic matter or to kill cells (Ma et al., 2021; Titchou et al., 2021). AOPs have conventionally used oxidants (e.g., ozone or hydrogen peroxide), UV irradiation, or Fe3+/Fe2+ (as homogeneous catalyst in Fenton reaction), among other techniques. However, heterogeneous photocatalytic oxidation has proven to be a more efficient approach, markedly accelerating the production of ROS under irradiation with UV or visible light. Photocatalysis is also a more sustainable procedure in comparison to homogenous AOPs, because heterogeneous photocatalysts can be recovered for reutilization by multiple mechanical processes, including sedimentation, membrane separation, centrifugation, filtration, or even, in the case of paramagnetic materials, the application of magnetic fields. Nevertheless, the post-degradation recovery of nanoparticulate photocatalysts has proven to be challenging, hampering their application at an industrial scale, although novel techniques are being developed to overcome this limitation, such as membrane-assisted photocatalyst recovery (Ravi and Golder, 2025).

Among photocatalytic materials developed to purify water, nanocomposites are one of the most attractive options due to the formation of heterojunctions, whose advantages were discussed above. VOSviewer (version 1.6.20) was used to conduct a scientometric analysis of research trends over the past 10 years on the application of MO-NPs with carbon-based nanocomposites in photocatalytic water treatment. Fig. 7 , developed using the Scopus database, plots the network of key words as nodes over the past 10 years.

Fig. 7a shows that the most frequent MO node was TiO2, followed by ZnO. The strength of connections between these two MOs is greater in the studies, reflecting their usefulness as key components in photocatalytic wastewater treatments under visible irradiation. As can be seen, nickel and copper are the most recently investigated MOs, followed by ferric and bismuth oxides. Notably, investigation into the application of UV irradiation has been virtually replaced by research on solar/visible irradiation over the past eight years. With regard to the field of application, the strongest connection of the nanocomposites is with the node represented by wastewater treatments, although there has been a recent increase in their antibacterial activity.

Fig. 7b demonstrates that GR nanocomposites are the photocatalysts most frequently researched for wastewater treatment, highlighting recent interest in their elimination of pathogenic microorganisms and capacity for utilization under both UV and solar irradiation. CNTs are the second most frequently studied photocatalysts for wastewater treatment under UV irradiation, but no many research has yet been published on their disinfection and antibacterial activities. Over the past two years, there has been an exponential growth in studies on the photocatalytic activity of CQDs in carbon nanocomposites, mainly related to their antibacterial action under solar/visible irradiation.

Comparison of Fig. 7a and 7b indicate increased investigation into the combination of GR with TiO2 and ZnO in photocatalytic nanocomposites, with a rise in research on its combination with NiO over the past four years. Color gradients show a current trend for the combination of CQD-based nanocomposites with ferric oxide or copper oxide in water treatment research, while combinations with fullerenes or tungsten no longer appear to attract research interest. The capacity of systems under visible light is at the heart of the network, being a key requirement for both wastewater treatment and pathogen inactivation.

Over the past few decades, there has been considerable research on the utilization of MOs as photocatalysts for water remediation, and a large number of recent publications have offered an overall overview on the removal of numerous types of water pollutants (Díaz et al., 2022; Ikram et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2016b). Specifically, reviews have addressed their application for the photodegradation of drugs (Velempini et al., 2021), dyes (Kumari et al., 2023), pesticides (Alhalili, 2023), and even microplastics (Kumar, 2023). Reviews have also focused on the usefulness of particular MOs to photodegrade organic pollutants in water, including Fe2O3 (Zhou et al., 2023), NiO (Akbari et al., 2020), SnO2 (do Nascimento et al., 2022; Roy et al., 2023), WO3 (Mohd Razali et al., 2021; Yuju et al., 2023), ZnO (Liu et al., 2019b; Nawaz et al., 2024), and TiO2 (Arun et al., 2023; Riaz and Park, 2020). In this regard, a promising approach for the effective photocatalytic removal of water pollutants is the synthesis of nanocomposites with CNMs, because of their larger surface area, efficient light absorption, and enhanced charge carrier separation.

Tables 1–4
display examples of the application of MOs/CNM in AOPS, detailing the mechanism of the photocatalytic degradation in each case. It can be observed that HO• and O2 •– radicals are the more commonly generated ROS. Besides O2 •– and HO•, other species can play a major role in the photodegradation of contaminants. For instance, photogenerated h
+ have been reported to act as the main species directly degrading the following: rhodamine B (RhB) when utilizing nanocomposites of nitrogen-doped TiO2 and Fe3O4 with CQDs (Chen et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2020a); the drug diclofenac when using nanocomposites of bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl)/Bi2O3 with CNTs (Sone et al., 2024); or MB and nitrophenol when using a nanocomposite of Ag-loaded WO3 particles in rGO (Vanasundari et al., 2024). Falara et al. observed that photoinduced e
– were responsible for nitrophenol degradation when TiO2 films with CQDs were used as photocatalyst (Falara et al., 2023). Chen et al. (2020) also found that photoinduced e
– and h
+ directly degraded 2,2′,4,4′-tetrabromodiphenyl ether (BDE47) in a Cu2O-rGO-TiO2 nanocomposite (Chen et al., 2020).

A brief account of the mechanism underlying the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants in water using MO/CNM composites will be followed by discussion of organic pollutant removal using MO nanocomposites with each CNM described in this review. Among carbon materials, fullerenes (C60) have very favorable optoelectronic properties and diverse structural and morphological features for the preparation of promising and versatile nanocomposites. Nevertheless, there are some drawbacks to their application (Pan et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2023; Yao et al., 2020), including: (1) the relatively high cost and difficulty of the traditional fullerene synthesis method; (2) the inadequacies of studies on the photocatalytic mechanism of the composites, which requires further research; and (3) the usually nonoptimal interface contact between MO and fullerene. These limitations explain the absence of fullerene from the list in Fig. 7b of the most frequently used materials for nanocomposite preparation. There has also been less research on the utilization in AOPs of MO/fullerene nanocomposites in comparison to other carbonaceous materials. The dearth of publications on fullerene/carbonaceous nanocomposites over the past five years is why Table 1 exhibits examples of contaminant removal from 2008 rather than from 2019, which is the case for other carbonaceous materials. In fact, applications of fullerene/MO nanocomposites have focused on the photodegradation of dyes (e.g., MB or RhB) as model pollutants rather than the removal of persistent organic pollutants (Pan et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2020). Thus, only three of the ten studies in Table 1 provide information on the removal of organic pollutants found in wastewaters, including the antimicrobial sulfathiazole (Wang et al., 2015), the herbicide mesotrione (Djordjevic et al., 2018), and the antibiotic norfloxacin (Behera et al., 2019). Two main approaches have been published for the synthesis of these nanocomposites. The first is the impregnation method, in which commercial fullerenes and MOs (solid or precursors) are dissolved in water or organic solvents (toluene, benzene, or tetrahydrofuran), and ultrasound or solvent reflux is then applied (Djordjevic et al., 2018; Fu et al., 2008; Soo Park and Bae Ko, 2015). The second approach is the hydrothermal method, in which fullerenes are oxidized with strong acids (sulfuric, nitric or n-chloroperoxybenzoic acids) before its introduction with the MO precursors in autoclaves, ensuring a correct anchoring of MOs on their surface (Behera et al., 2019; Long et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2015). Other approaches have been adopted by some authors. Thus, Navgire and Lande obtained C60-doped MoO3-TiO2 nanocomposites by the sol-gel method (Navgire and Lande, 2017), and Wu et al. (2021) prepared a photoactive nanocomposite (Zinc coproporphyrin [ZnCPP]-Fullerol@TiO2, Fig. 8 ) to anchor fullerene-cored star-shaped polyporphyrins on the surface of TiO2 NPs (Wu et al., 2021).

CQDs have attracted increasing interest over the years for biomedical applications due to their low toxicity, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, high dispersibility, and tunable luminescence (Chen et al., 2024). MO composites of CQDs are therefore candidates as photocatalysts for water remediation (see publication trend in Fig. 7b), being known to effectively contribute to the separation of the positive h
+ and e
– through their excellent e
– transfer and reservoir capacities. CQDs can be prepared using several natural carbon resources, but fabrication methods are not yet well established, and further research is needed on the preparation of cost-effective nanocomposites (Beker et al., 2022; Rani et al., 2020; Tripti et al., 2024). Table 2 lists some recent studies that used MO/CQD nanocomposites to photodegrade organic water pollutants, including dyes and pharmaceutical drugs such as propranolol, atenolol (Ponkshe and Thakur, 2022); TC (John and Mathew, 2023), and phenazopyridine (Bazazi et al., 2023) as well as 2,4-D and diuron herbicides (John and Mathew, 2023). The CQDs described in the table were synthesized by the hydrothermal method except for those prepared by Ponkshe & Thakur using graphite rods and electrophoretic dissolution (Ponkshe and Thakur, 2022). The most straightforward approach to synthesize MO/CQD nanocomposites is by the impregnation method, in which magnetic stirring and/or sonication is applied to a suspension of the CQDs and MOs (Jamila et al., 2020; Montazer et al., 2023; Nugraha et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2020a). Other authors introduced the CQDs and MO precursor into an autoclave as a hydrothermal approach to ensure anchoring of the MOs onto the surface of the CQDs (Bazazi et al., 2023; Hidayat et al., 2024; John and Mathew, 2023; Ponkshe and Thakur, 2022). Interestingly, Falara et al. prepared films of TiO2/CQD composite by dip-coating a conductive fluorine-doped tin oxide glass substrate in a sol-gel solution containing TiO2 precursors (or commercial Degussa P25) and CQDs (Falara et al., 2023). By way of an example, Fig. 9 displays the synthesis and application of the cuttlebone powder-modified CQD/NiO prepared by Montazer et al. for propranolol and atenolol degradation, including SEM micrographs (Montazer et al., 2023).

Over the past decade, CNTs have been of major research interest for the preparation of MO composites due to their mechanical strength, electronic and thermal stability, capacity to transport molecules, and biocompatibility. SWCNTs have been less well studied in comparison to MWCNTs for various reasons, including: their poor dispersion in water and formation of agglomerations due to strong π−π and van der Waals interactions; their surface inertness; low adsorption efficiency; and difficulties in their separation from aqueous media. Furthermore, MWCNTs are easier to produce and less expensive and, crucially, their outer wall can be chemically modified to endow materials with a wide range of properties without compromising the special properties of CNTs, because the inner walls remain intact. On the other hand, SWCNTs have proven highly useful in such fields as photovoltaics and communications, while surface-modified MWCNTs have been extensively used as adsorbents of pollutants (e.g., dyes and antibiotics) from aqueous media (Li et al., 2022b; Mashkoor et al., 2020; Mousavi and Janjani, 2020). There has been less research on modified CNTs as photocatalysts than on their role as adsorbents in wastewater treatments. Table 3 lists studies in which MO/CNT composites were found to be efficient photocatalysts to eliminate dyes and different pharmaceutical drugs, such as the antibiotic TC (Isari et al., 2020; Varghese et al., 2023), antimicrobial sulfathiazole (Hayati et al., 2020), and anti-inflammatory diclofenac (Sone et al., 2024). Isari et al. and Hayati et al. achieved the efficient mineralization of pollutants in pharmaceutical wastewaters by means of sono-photocatalysis with WO3/CNT and N, Fe co-doped TiO 2 @SWCNT nanocomposites, respectively (Hayati et al., 2020; Isari et al., 2020). All MO/CNT synthesis studies in Table 3 used commercial CNTs except for the investigation by Majeed Khan et al., who synthesized their own SWCNT by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (Majeed Khan et al., 2019). Most of these studies used CNTs as provided, while some authors included an oxidation step that used strong acids (nitric acid [HNO3] and sulfuric acid [H2SO4]) to create functional groups on the CNT surface for reaction with the MO precursors (Li et al., 2020a; Sone et al., 2024; Wani et al., 2024).
