Nanocomposites were obtained by the impregnation method in some of the studies, directly mixing MOs and CNTs by magnetic stirring and sonication (Abo-Dief et al., 2022; Devi et al., 2023; Mishra et al., 2024; Udrescu et al., 2021), whereas others applied the hydrothermal method (Isari et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a; Varghese et al., 2023; Wani et al., 2024). In other approaches, Majeed Khan et al. decorated Cu2O NPs with SWCNT at low temperature by the radio frequency-sputtering method (Majeed Khan et al., 2019), and Sone et al. synthesized a BiOCl/Bi2O3/MWCNT nanocomposite by mixing MWCNT and BiOCl in a solution and then adding reagents for consecutive reduction and oxidation steps (Sone et al., 2024). Fig. 10 depicts SEM micrographs of the BiOCl/Bi2O3/MWCNT nanocomposites, stability tests of photocatalytic diclofenac degradation, and the proposed photodegradation mechanism.

Over the past two decades, despite being the most recently discovered allotropic form of carbon, GR has attracted much more interest in the field of water treatment than have fullerenes or CNTs. There have been frequent reviews on the use of GO or rGO/MO nanocomposites to remove organic pollutants (Dutta et al., 2019; Hong et al., 2020; Karim and Selvaraj, 2021; Li et al., 2022a) because of their multiple advantages over other carbon materials, including their greater mechanical stiffness, optical transparency, and higher electrical and thermal conductivity. Nevertheless, the most relevant characteristic of GR in the formation of nanocomposites with MOs is its large surface area (up to 2600 m g−1), which improves interfacial contact, enhances e
– mobility, increases the adsorption of organic pollutants, and avoids the agglomeration of particles in the nanocomposite. Table 4 lists recently published studies on the utilization of GO and rGO/MO composites as photocatalysts to remove organic water pollutants, such as the disinfectant 2, 4-dimethyl phenol (Shandilya et al., 2020), flame retardant BDE47 (Chen et al., 2020), anticonvulsive drug carbamazepine (Fu and Gray, 2021), antibiotics oxytetracycline and sulfamethoxazole (SMX) (Sanei et al., 2023), food colorant tartrazine (Torres-Hernández et al., 2024), and several types of dyes. The GO utilized in these nanocomposites was mainly synthesized by applying modifications of the well-established Hummer's method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958), in which the oxidant potassium permanganate (KMnO4) is added to a solution containing graphite powder and strong acids (sodium nitrate [NaNO3] and H2SO4), followed by ultrasonic exfoliation. Commercial GO was used by some researchers (Chen et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2022), while Yousaf et al. applied Tour's method (Yousaf et al., 2021), which is a less toxic and safer than Hummer's method, with a lower environmental impact, because a fraction of the H2SO4 is replaced with phosphoric acid (Marcano et al., 2010). Although many studies (6 out of 16 references) synthesized MO/GO or rGO nanocomposites by the hydrothermal method (Jiang et al., 2022; Mehr Un et al., 2024; Sanei et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021; Yousaf et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2022) a wider range of approaches have been used for their synthesis in comparison to fullerene, CNT, and CQD nanocomposites. For instance, Chen et al. (2020) painted Cu2O films with an alcoholic dispersion of hydrothermal-synthesized rGO-TiO2 nanocomposites, which were then annealed under N2 atmosphere to prepare Cu2O-(rGO-TiO2) films (Chen et al., 2020). In another study, Botsa et al. obtained SnO2-Fe2O3-rGO nanocomposites by applying magnetic stirring and solvent evaporation to a solution containing iron nitrate, SnO2, and rGO (Botsa et al., 2020). Likewise, Danish et al. (2022) used the impregnation method to prepare a Fe2O3- g-C3N4-GO nanocomposite by the magnetic stirring of suspension containing their components (Danish et al., 2022). AlAnazi et al. deposited sol-gel synthesized-NiO and MnO2 on GR-coated nickel foams by means of spin-coating and electrodeposition, respectively (AlAnazi et al., 2023). Torres et al. prepared films of TiO2-Bi2O3-rGO nanocomposites by dip-coating a glass slide substrate with solutions containing TiO2 and Bi2O3 precursors and 2 wt% of rGO (Torres-Hernández et al., 2024). Interestingly, Vanasundari et al. synthesized an Ag-doped WO3/rGO nanocomposite using an extract of pomegranate (Punica Granatum). The extract was added to a suspension containing GO, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), tungstic acid (H2WO4), and silver nitrate (AgNO3), which was then heated under reflux at 80 °C for 24 h (Vanasundari et al., 2024). Finally, Huong et al. prepared a nanocomposite of WO3, rGO, and the metal-organic-framework Ui66@ by the solvothermal method, with the novel approach of using the WO3-Zirconium 1,4-dicarboxybenzene metal-organic framework (MOF) (UiO-66)@rGO nanocomposite as a 3D printing material by mixing 5 or 10 wt% with a printing resin (Huong et al., 2024). Fig. 11 illustrates a scheme of the 3D-printed WO3-UiO-66@rGO nanocomposites, showing their SEM micrographs and performance in the photocatalytic removal of SMX.

This overview of carbon materials used for water remediation ends with a comparative analysis of the information in Tables 1–4 GO composites have been prepared using a wider variety of approaches and have been combined with a larger number of MOs in comparison to other carbon materials (see Fig. 7b). Furthermore, GO/rGO nanocomposites have been used to photodegrade more types of pollutants, while other carbon composites have mainly served to remove dyes. Importantly, GO/rGO and CQDs are easy to prepare and synthesize at a relatively low cost, whereas the CNT and C60 used by the studies cited in Tables 2 and 3, respectively, were almost always purchased, limiting the functionalization and application of these carbon materials. TiO2 has been the most selected MO for preparing carbon nanocomposites (see Fig. 7a). There has been a trend towards the utilization of solar light rather than UV or visible light irradiation in photodegradation processes, and Fig. 7a shows that the most frequent keyword in recent publications is “solar light irradiation”, followed by “visible-light irradiation” and then “UV-light irradiation.

As detailed in the previous section, the utilization of carbon/MO nanocomposites as photocatalysts to remove organic pollutants from wastewater is a thriving and promising field of study to confront water shortages. Photocatalysis is also becoming an attractive technology for water disinfection, having the potential to simultaneously eliminate microorganisms and organic water pollutants (Shandilya et al., 2020). In comparison to ozonation or chlorination, photocatalysis markedly increases the production of ROS to destroy cells, and it can be designed to use sunlight instead of expensive sources of UV (Dalrymple et al., 2010).

An increasing number of studies have been published on the application of MO composites for water disinfection. NPs of a wide range of MOs have exhibited antibacterial activity due to their chemical composition, shape, size, and other properties, which induce chemical and physical alterations in bacteria. Although the antibacterial activity of MOs has yet to be fully elucidated, it has been associated with the following three processes (Franco et al., 2022; Stankic et al., 2016): (a) a bacteriostatic effect, with suppression of bacterial reproduction/growth when positively-charged NPs are attached by electrostatic charge to the negatively-charged membrane or wall of the cells, causing their dysfunction, while other MOs deform or pierce the membrane or cell walls by physical interaction, killing the bacteria; (b), the internalization of MO-NPs in bacteria, depending on the size and chemical affinity of the NPs, which alters the metabolism of the cell; (c) the release of metal ions that can trespass cell membranes and disrupt essential metabolic pathways of bacteria; and (d) the post-irradiation generation of charge carriers that react with O2, H2O, or other species to produce cytotoxic ROS, which attack the cell membrane, proteins, or nucleic acids, killing the cell. The utilization of MOs to generate ROS has proven to be an efficient procedure not only to prevent the growth of bacteria but also to completely eliminate fungi and viruses, among other microorganisms (Pachaiappan et al., 2021; Prakash et al., 2022).

As already noted, the antibacterial activity of MO-NPs is dependent on their properties. For instance, a study of ZnO NPs (Qi et al., 2017) concluded that: (a) smaller particle sizes provide higher surface area for the more extensive attachment of bacteria; (b) rods, flower-like, and nanowire morphologies more easily penetrate the membrane of cells in comparison to spherically-shaped NPs; and (c) antibacterial activity is greater with higher NP concentration and the resulting increase in ROS production. These observations are also valid for TiO2, another MO frequently employed in disinfection processes (Prakash et al., 2022). Like ZnO, the antibacterial activity of TiO2 is intrinsically tied to its photocatalytic properties and the generation of ROS (Thakur et al., 2024).

The strong antibacterial activity of CNMs is also attributable to their hydrophobicity, which allows the penetration of cell membranes and can produce ROS upon irradiation. The antibacterial activities of GO and rGO have been extensively documented (Dutta et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2020) and associated with the oxidative stress caused by ROS generation and, less strongly, to physical cuts and lipid extraction involving the cell membrane (Maas, 2016). Although greater oxidative stress is generated by rGO than by GO, due to its higher electrical conduction, GO has been reported to exert higher antibacterial activity through the presence of more hydrophilic functional groups on its surface, improving its dispersion and cell contact (Liu et al., 2011). CQDs have also attracted attention as potential bactericidal materials thanks to their ready preparation, controlled size, high water dispersibility, biocompatibility, and modifiability of their germicidal range (Zhao et al., 2023). The size, hydrophilicity, and positive charge of CQDs mean that they excel in penetrating and destroying not only the negatively charged adenosine phosphate cell walls of Gram-positive bacteria but also the negatively charged phospholipids of the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria (Yang et al., 2016). SWCNTs were also found to pierce the surface of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, causing leakage of their internal contents and death, observing greater antibacterial efficiency with longer versus shorter CNTs (Mocan et al., 2017). Finally, fullerenes are not as effective as bactericides in comparison to other carbon allotropes because they do not alter bacterial membranes, although their strong photocatalytic activity produces a high proportion of cytotoxic ROS (Maas, 2016).

Therefore, as in the case of pollutant photodegradation, the disinfection capacity of MO/CNM composites is greater than that of their individual components. This is because their heterojunctions offer efficient charge carrier separation to produce ROS that eliminate microorganisms via oxidative stress (Pachaiappan et al., 2021). The bactericidal properties of MO/CNM nanocomposites are reported in Table 5 , which includes recently published studies in which MOs and C60, CQD, CNT, and GO/rGO nanocomposites are tested against microorganisms. As in the previous section, the most frequently selected MO is TiO2, followed by ZnO, and the most frequently used carbon material is GO/rGO, followed by CQD. Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. Coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were the most widely used bacteria to elucidate the antibacterial activity of photocatalysts. Other microorganisms that were disinfected include: (a) Gram-negative bacteria Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) (Mohan et al., 2020; Prema et al., 2020; Wagh et al., 2024), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) (AlAnazi et al., 2023; Prema et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2023), Shigella flexneri (S. flexneri) (Prema et al., 2020), and Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris) (Wagh et al., 2024); (b) Gram-positive bacteria Candida albicans (C. albicans) (AlAnazi et al., 2023), Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) (Singh et al., 2023; Wagh et al., 2024), and Bacillus Subtilis (B. Subtilis) (Vanasundari et al., 2024); and (c) Fungi Bipolaris sorokiniana (B. sorokiniana) (Wagh et al., 2024), Fusarium graminearum Schwabe (F. g. Schwabe), Colletotrichum gloeosporioides Penz (C. g. Penz), Botryosphaeria dothidea (B. dothidea), Fusarium moniliforme Sheld (F. m. Sheld), and Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. Vasinfectum (F. o. f. sp. Vasinfectum) (Zhang et al., 2021).

Some results of MO/CNM nanocomposites reported in Table 5 are worth highlighting. For instance, Mohan et al. (2020) prepared solvothermal-synthesized GO/V2O5 nanocomposites doped with 1 wt% of platinum NPs and achieved S. typhimurium disinfection close to 8.2 log colony forming-unit (CFU), attributed to their prolonged e
– lifespan compared with V2O5 alone, and they proved highly effective against B16F10 melanoma cancer cells (Mohan et al., 2020). Prema et al. used growth curve profiling, well diffusion, biofilm inhibition, colony counting and hemolysis analysis to measure the antibacterial activity of GO/ZnO composites against Gram-negative E. coli, S. typhimurium, P. aeruginosa, and S. flexneri bacteria. The nanocomposite inhibited the bacterial growth of all four species, and the disinfection efficiency was greater under light irradiation and with higher concentrations of nanocomposites, indicating that ROS generation was the main disinfection mechanism (Prema et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2021) designed a photocatalysis for the elimination of fungi and bacteria by decorating titanium ion (Ti3+) self-doped TiO2 with CQDs and palladium. After 30 min under visible light irradiation, the antibacterial activity against E. coli was ⁓6.5-fold higher for the Pd/CQDs/Ti3+-TiO2 nanocomposite with 1 wt% palladium than for pure TiO2. This composite also proved highly efficacious in the photo-disinfection of five pathogenic agricultural fungi. These outcomes were attributed to the synergetic effect of Pd/CQDs and Ti3+-TiO2, with the former trapping photoinduced electrons in the CB of the latter, causing a high production of cytotoxic h
+ , O2 •–, HO•, and e
– radical species (Zhang et al., 2021). Finally, Wagh et al. tested CNTs loaded with 5 wt% Ag-doped ZnO NPs (Ag-ZnO/CNT) against B. sorokiniana fungi and obtained an optimum inhibition efficiency of 48 % for the composite prepared with 5 wt% CNT (5CNT). This was attributed to the photogenerated ROS and leached zinc ion (Zn2+), which disrupted the cell wall and reacted with proteins and nucleic acids of the fungi. The antibacterial activity of this composite was also evaluated against Gram-positive (S. aureus and B. cereus) and Gram-negative (P. vulgaris and S. typhimurium) bacteria, obtaining higher zone of inhibition (ZOI) values for all four bacteria with the composite than with the commercial antibiotic streptomycin (Wagh et al., 2024). Fig. 12 depicts: FE-SEM micrographs of Ag-ZnO/CNT nanocomposites synthesized with different amounts of CNT; the antifungal activity of 5CNT tested by agar diffusion; and the antibacterial activity of all synthesized composites tested by agar diffusion.

This review offers a comprehensive overview on the application of carbon-based MO nanocomposites for water treatment, including pollutant degradation and disinfection. Photocatalysts can be optimized by the combination of materials, controlling charge transfer and light absorption and improving dispersion and stability. Due to their character as semiconductors and their NP size, the carbon materials most frequently used for photocatalysis are quantum dots, fullerenes, nanotubes, and GR. MO semiconductors have been extensively explored as photocatalysts for water remediation, and this remains a relevant line of research. MOs can act as a photocatalyst when they are capable to generate the relocation of electrons and holes after the absorption of light with an intensity not lower than the bandgap value of the semiconductor. The heterojunction structures formed in carbon-based MO nanocomposites create an intrinsic electric field that drives e
– diffusion across the interface. This review of the roles of commonly applied MO-NPs describes the mechanisms by which this electric field and band structure influence the charge carrier dynamics.

Scientometric analysis of publication trends in photocatalytic nanocomposites for water treatment reveals an uplift in research on: (i) the combination of GR with TiO2, ZnO, and, more recently, NiO, (ii) nanocomposites that combine CQDs with ferric or copper oxide for water treatment, and (iii) the utilization of visible irradiation for wastewater treatment and pathogen inactivation.

Combinations of carbon materials such as GR and CQD with MOs such as TiO2 show major promise as photocatalytic semiconductors for water remediation. GO and rGO, which offer unique properties and superior performances, have been increasingly combined with a larger number of MOs to photodegrade a wider variety of pollutants, whereas there is a relative loss of interest in fullerenes and CNTs. Another current trend in photodegradation is to use solar rather than UV or visible light irradiation.

A promising development is the use of carbon-based MO nanocomposites as photocatalysts for water disinfection. When exposed to radiation, they generate cytotoxic free radicals that attack and kill microorganisms. GO/rGO has demonstrated excellent antibacterial performance, and CQDs have also proven useful, offering the benefits of controllable size, high dispersibility in water, and biocompatibility.

Future research is urgently needed on the following key areas:
•
The design and synthesis of novel cost-effective materials with improved properties and scalability to meet water treatment challenges.
•
The performance of carbon-based MO nanocomposites in photocatalytic treatments for natural water under realistic conditions, following up promising laboratory results with pilot testing to explore their scaling up.
•
The stability and reusability of these nanocomposites under irradiation by testing their repeated performance under realistic operating conditions.
•
The exploration of novel combinations of MOs with carbon materials in the development of nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic activity. The combination and synergistic effects of these carbon-based MO nanocomposites will significantly advance the joint photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants and bacterial inactivation.
•
Advances in the application of carbon-based MO nanocomposites for natural water treatment with elucidation of underlying reaction mechanisms for a holistic approach to treatment, accelerating the real-world application of these materials.
•
Design of effective reactors with optimized solar light irradiation distribution, mass transfer, and catalyst recovery to address real-world scalability obstacles.
•
Evaluation of the toxicity and environmental impact of treatments, ensuring their safe application.

It can be expected that these nanocomposites, with their special properties and characteristics, will be manufactured in a sustainable and cost-effective manner in the future. They represent a potential solution to multiple problems related to water scarcity and the environment. This review highlights the need for further integrated research into synergistic aspects of pollutant removal and water disinfection.
